data.title: Give officials more power to probe suspected animal cruelty cases: watchdog
data.description:
Hong Kong’s ombudsman has urged the government to empower conservation officers to enter people’s homes to investigate suspected animal cruelty cases, describing existing procedures as “ineffective” with less than 1 per cent of reported cases resulting in prosecutions.
A report released on Thursday on the Office of the Ombudsman’s latest direct investigation operation also called for strengthening penalties against illegal animal traps, describing current fines as “no deterrence” against the deadly devices.
“As the authority overseeing animal management and welfare, the Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department [AFCD] must handle every report conscientiously, diligently and promptly, yet our investigation reveals several systemic problems in the AFCD’s approach,” Ombudsman Jack Chan Jick-chi told a press briefing on the findings.
“The investigative approach is ineffective against offenders intending to conceal evidence, and it directly undermines the effectiveness in pursuing prosecutions,” he said in an accompanying press release.
Chan said the government watchdog received a record 3,000 submissions related to its investigation, reflecting “great public concern” over the topic.
According to official data shared by the office, the number of reports of suspected animal cruelty cases received by the AFCD nearly doubled between 2020 and 2024, from 215 to 423.
But from the 1,633 reports handled by the department between 2020 and the first half of 2025, only six resulted in prosecutions – or just 0.4 per cent. Another 10 cases referred to the AFCD by other government departments also resulted in prosecutions.
As part of the investigation, Chan said, his team examined the department’s procedures for handling reported animal cruelty, conducted on-site inspections and reviewed 200 randomly selected case files.

The office found a number of cases where officers could not access a flat to investigate suspected animal abuse.
Under the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance, AFCD officers can take action against suspected animal cruelty but cannot enter premises unless they get the occupant’s express consent. The department also cannot seek court warrants for enforcement.
In some cases, officers relied solely on observations made outside a flat or based their decision on a single telephone call with the occupant.
In other instances, cases were closed “prematurely” without confirming whether animal cruelty had occurred.
The office also found that while the department had specific guidelines for handling cases involving a large number of animals, it only began formulating guidelines for general cases after the ombudsman’s investigation started.
Among 45 recommendations, the office called on authorities to amend the ordinance to introduce procedures for allowing department staff to enter premises without needing the occupant’s consent.
The office also called for stiffer penalties against those who illegally possessed and used animal traps.
It noted that the current maximum penalty was a fine of up to HK$50,000 with no risk of jail time. By comparison, those found illegally feeding pigeons faced a fine of up to HK$100,000 and one year in prison.
“While the two offences are of a different nature, the severe risks posed by animal traps to both animals and the public cannot be ignored,” Chan said.
“A fine alone fails to reflect the seriousness of the offence and provides no deterrence.”
In a statement, the department said the government had “always attached great importance to animal welfare and deters acts of cruelty to animals through stringent enforcement and preventive education initiatives”.
It said it accepted the office’s recommendations would actively follow up on its recommendations.
“The government has been conducting research on the amendment of the ordinance and will strive to provide a concrete update within this year.”

data.title: Hongkongers as young as 6 should ‘feel proud to be Chinese’: learning guide
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities expect students as young as six to “feel proud to be Chinese” and to have a basic understanding of national affairs, according to a revamped guide on values education stressing patriotism.
The 2026 Values Education Curriculum Framework, published by the Education Bureau on Thursday, applies to all government, aided, special and private schools, as well as those under the Direct Subsidy Scheme.
The framework was prepared by the Standing Committee on Values Education, which falls under the Curriculum Development Council, and was intended to serve as a guiding document for schools to plan and implement the values education curriculum, authorities said.
It places a significantly heavier emphasis on “national identity” – one of the 12 values outlined in the document – compared with the 2021 version.
The other values are perseverance, respect for others, responsibility, commitment, integrity, benevolence, law-abidingness, empathy, diligence, filial piety and unity.
The latest guide calls for schools to deepen students’ understanding of China’s constitution, the Basic Law, national security education and the “one country, two systems” governing principle.
According to the framework, Primary One to Three students, who are typically six to eight years old, should “gain a basic understanding of national affairs” and “feel proud to be Chinese”.
Primary Four to Six students, meanwhile, are expected to understand the importance of national unity and ethnic solidarity, and should learn to safeguard national security.
“They should understand the significant achievements and latest developments under the leadership of the Communist Party, cultivate an awareness of safeguarding the common interests of the country and the people, and gradually establish a national identity,” it said.

The guide said that students in Form One to Three, usually those aged about 12 to 14, are expected to understand the importance of safeguarding national security and the country’s political system and “the leading role of the Communist Party”.
Senior secondary students should be willing to shoulder the responsibility of “improving the well-being of the country and the people”, and “love the country and Hong Kong”, according to the document.
“They should actively learn about the country’s overall development, and through learning, exchange and cooperation, actively contribute to the construction of Hong Kong and the great rejuvenation of the Chinese nation,” it said.
The new guide also calls for teachers to stand firm and tell students that there is no room for discussion on certain big issues.
“For issues that are ‘black and white’, which involve clear distinctions between right and wrong, and well-defined legal principles, teachers should clearly list facts from historical, moral, legal and constitutional perspectives,” it said.
“Teachers should point out to students that there is no room for discussion or compromise on these issues, and that facts should not be arbitrarily distorted. This will ensure students have a correct understanding of the issues.”
The framework also lays out expectations that teachers should guide students to understand the bottom line, distinguish right from wrong, and gradually establish “correct” values if students deviate or become confused.
“Teachers should promptly point them out and correct them,” it noted.
Under the framework, schools should arrange for pupils to watch patriotic and anti-war films to help foster a sense of national identity.
Aside from teaching various subjects, educators are also expected to encourage students to read historical books and the biographies of notable figures to boost their love for the country.
The Education Bureau has said it will organise an online briefing next month and another in September so teachers can learn more about the new guide.

data.title: Black market workarounds scale up for Claude as Anthropic tightens ID checks
data.description:
US artificial intelligence start-up Anthropic’s move to introduce real-name identity verification has triggered alarm in China, raising the prospect that access to its tools could be cut off entirely and prompting black-market vendors to scramble for workarounds.
The shift highlights the strong demand for Anthropic’s Claude models in China, despite an official ban that also covers Hong Kong and Macau. The company said the restrictions were necessary for national security reasons amid intensifying US-China competition in AI.
Anthropic quietly updated its user policy on Wednesday, requiring users to submit government-issued identification alongside a live selfie to “prevent abuse, enforce our usage policies, and comply with legal obligations”.
The change quickly reverberated across China’s developer community, where many programmers rely on Claude and its flagship coding product, Claude Code, due to their advanced capabilities.
While there are no official figures for Anthropic’s user base in China, access has typically been maintained through virtual private servers that mask location, or via relay platforms such as AICodeMirror, which says it has more than 10,000 registered users and over 200 institutional clients.
The new requirements – akin to “know your customer” checks more commonly seen in the financial services industry – are expected to make such access far more difficult.
A Shenzhen engineer surnamed Zhang said he had been using Claude “for years” as he had a phone number, credit card and IP address registered in Singapore, where he previously lived. However, as he only had Chinese identification documents, he was unlikely to be able to bypass the checks this time around, he said.
Under the updated rules, users must present original government-issued identification – such as a passport or driving licence – held physically during verification, rather than submitting copies or digital versions.

As Zhang’s Claude account remained active, he said he was being careful with usage to avoid triggering platform checks while also exploring the use of other models such as OpenAI’s GPT and Google’s Gemini.
While both of these other US models are also officially unavailable in China, neither OpenAI nor Google has introduced real-name identification checks.
On Chinese social media, adverts claiming to offer workarounds to the new rules have already appeared.
Approached by the South China Morning Post, a vendor on popular second-hand e-commerce platform Xianyu claimed to have access to “tens of thousands” of people in Africa who could handle the verification process for a fee of 150 yuan (US$22) per user.
It remains unclear how sustainable such workarounds will be, particularly as Anthropic continues to monitor user activity after registration.
Even before the policy update, some Chinese users had reported being prompted midsession to verify their identities, sharing screenshots online.
Anthropic said it was working with San Francisco-based security firm Persona to implement the new system. According to Persona’s website, its verification services covered more than 200 countries and regions, including China, where it could check personal details against official databases.
Last week, Anthropic unveiled Claude Mythos Preview, releasing it only to a consortium of major US companies, citing cybersecurity risks.
Speaking in Washington on Tuesday, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent described Mythos as a breakthrough in the US-China AI race.
Additional reporting by Ben Jiang

data.title: Owner of luxury Hong Kong home fined HK$110,000 over illegal structures, pool
data.description:
The owner of a luxury home at a seaside estate in Hong Kong has been fined HK$110,000 (US$14,039) for building illegal structures and a swimming pool following a government crackdown triggered by a record-breaking rainstorm and an investigation by the South China Morning Post.
Sunset Boulevard Company, which owns House No 76 at Redhill Peninsula, the luxury low-rise estate in Tai Tam, was sentenced at Eastern Court on Thursday.
The private firm, founded in 1995, lists Hong Kong resident Marc Robert Compagnon as its director, according to Companies Registry records.
Principal Magistrate David Cheung Chi-wai heard that the unauthorised additions on the second and fourth floors and in the garden, as well as the pool, had been in place since 2006.
The defence counsel noted the company’s directors lived there and argued in mitigation that the defendant had pleaded guilty at the earliest opportunity.
The court heard that the homeowner had already applied to the Buildings Department to remove the illegal structures, with work under way.
Cheung accepted that the company had “made an effort” to make amends.
He passed down fines ranging between HK$15,000 and HK$35,000 on each of the five summonses and ordered the company to pay a total penalty of HK$110,000 within 28 days.
The irregularities at House No 76 were first discovered by authorities after landslides, triggered by a record-breaking downpour in 2023, exposed extensive illegal additions at the seaside estate.
The SCMP in early 2024 exposed unauthorised modifications at the upmarket development including structures or extensions built on government land that increased the sizes of houses or gardens.
Four homes were suspected of having swimming pools unlisted in the government’s online records.
Authorities found 70 of the 85 seaside homes had extended beyond their legal limits, built unauthorised structures, or both.
The department in January this year said it had launched prosecutions against the owners of 30 detached houses on Redhill Peninsula, with 24 convictions so far.
Under the law, anyone convicted of carrying out building works without approval from the Buildings Department faces a maximum fine of HK$400,000 and two years in prison, plus a further penalty of HK$20,000 daily until rectified.
Hearings for other charged homes continue.

data.title: Hong Kong police arrest 5 over shoplifting syndicate targeting Muji stores
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested five people accused of stealing more than 445 items of clothing worth HK$113,000 (US$14,400) from multiple outlets of Japanese retail chain Muji.
Officers from Yau Tsim police district said they had received a string of shoplifting reports since earlier this year that involved large quantities of apparel taken from a retail chain, with a source naming the business as Muji.
The Japanese retail chain is known for its minimalist “no-brand quality goods” philosophy.
Police said detectives had identified a syndicate as being behind the thefts.
The group posted lookouts inside and outside the shops, while other members swiftly stuffed clothing into handbags and reusable bags before leaving, the force added.
Each theft lasted only a few minutes, with several outlets sometimes targeted on the same day.
Investigators found that the stolen goods were handed to other syndicate members on the same day as each theft, briefly stored in subdivided flats in Mong Kok or Sham Shui Po, then moved out within one to two days.
Suspects also disguised themselves by wearing surgical masks, hats, glasses and wigs to hinder identification.

On Tuesday, officers intercepted a man and two women after they allegedly delivered 70 stolen items from outlets in Yuen Long and Tsuen Wan to a flat in Mong Kok.
Police said they had recovered all of the items, which still had their price tags on.
The trio were arrested on suspicion of theft and handling stolen property.
Another man and a woman were also arrested in Stanley and Sham Shui Po on Wednesday and Thursday.
The force said the suspects were all Vietnamese nationals, aged 36 to 58, who held recognisance forms issued by the Immigration Department.
Such forms serve as temporary identification documents that allow holders to remain in Hong Kong but not to work.
Police said the syndicate was linked to 24 theft cases spanning from the middle of last year to this month, and all involving branches of the same retailer.
The force said the group was believed to have stolen more than 445 items of clothing worth HK$113,000 in total.
Three suspects have been charged with theft and will appear at Kowloon City Court on Friday. The remaining detainees are still under investigation.

data.title: TSMC targets over 30% revenue surge in 2026, ramps up capex amid AI boom
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co (TSMC) expects a revenue surge of more than 30 per cent in 2026 as the world’s largest contract chipmaker “pulls in all equipment” to ramp up supply, as management downplayed the impact of memory price hikes and Middle East tensions.
During its first-quarter earnings call on Thursday, company executives voiced optimism for the year ahead anchored by the global AI frenzy, attributing the expected revenue surge to strong demand for artificial intelligence and high performance computing (HPC) applications.
TSMC’s first-quarter revenue reached US$35.9 billion, a 40.6 per cent year-on-year increase that slightly surpassed the company’s own guidance.
To feed the insatiable appetite for AI chips, TSMC is aggressively ramping up its investments, especially in cutting-edge 3-nm chips. The company expects its 2026 capital expenditure to hit the high end of its record high US$52 billion to US$56 billion range.
That marked an acceleration compared with spending over the last three years, as expected 2026 expenditure would be more than half its total capex over the past three years, TSMC CEO C C Wei said on the earnings call. He added that capex in the next three years would be “significantly higher than the past three years”.

Despite the heavy investments, its wafer supply remains tight amid fierce customer competition to secure advanced capacity, especially in the area of advanced chip packaging.
To address the tight supply situation, Wei said TSMC was “pulling in all the equipment” it could to accelerate production. It is executing a global capacity plan for advanced 3-nm technologies, scheduling volume production at new plants in Tainan, Taiwan and the US state of Arizona in 2027.
The latest earnings also reflect a structural shift in TSMC’s revenue stream towards AI-driven applications, as revenue from HPC surged 20 per cent quarter on quarter to account for 61 per cent of the total, dwarfing the smartphone segment, which dropped 11 per cent to a share of just 26 per cent.
TSMC chief financial officer Wendell Huang forecast second-quarter revenue of between US$39 billion and US$40.2 billion, representing a 32 per cent year-on-year jump at the midpoint.
While the broader tech industry has expressed alarm over Middle East volatility and its impact on critical semiconductor materials like helium, TSMC management sought to reassure investors, saying disruptions to its operations were minimal.

Huang said the company did not expect any “near-term impact” on its operations from materials or energy supplies, pointing to TSMC’s strategy of maintaining multiple suppliers and holding a safety stock of helium and hydrogen.
Huang acknowledged, however, that prices for certain specialty chemicals were likely to increase, which could pose a risk to profitability, but added it was “too early to quantify the impact”.
Addressing the memory supply crunch that was adding cost pressure to consumer electronics, Wei said “memory price hikes definitely have some impact on price-sensitive markets, especially in the PC and smartphone market”.
Meanwhile, he expressed optimism for TSMC’s smartphone segment, noting that “high-end smartphones continue to do better”.

data.title: Rise in mental illness cases among Hong Kong students ‘reflects growing openness’
data.description:
Hong Kong’s education minister has said the surge in students diagnosed with mental illness partly reflects parents’ growing acceptance and openness about the issue, while academic pressures, social lives and physical health also play a role.
The remarks by Secretary for Education Christine Choi Yuk-lin came after the Education Bureau revealed that the number of secondary school students suffering from mental illness had doubled over the past five academic years, from 660 in 2020-21 to 1,330 in 2024-25.
In primary schools, the number of students diagnosed with mental illness also rose from 130 to 200 in the same period, a 54 per cent increase.
“I think [parents] have now adopted an attitude which is more open and accepting; therefore, so it is not a sudden surge,” Choi told lawmakers at a Legislative Council meeting on Thursday.
“It shows the [government’s] work has yielded some results, as parents are now willing to accept support services,” she said, adding that schools would receive grants to help students with special needs, including mental illness.
Choi conceded that parents in the past might have declined to admit that their children were mentally ill due to psychological pressures.
She said the causes of mental illness were complex and not due to one single factor.
“Reasons range from personal, family and academic pressure to social difficulties. Some may be physical or caused by brain secretions or a lack of certain elements, which is why we see all sorts of different cases,” she said.
She also said the government provided extensive training for parents of children with poor mental health or suicidal tendencies.

The bureau organises online workshops to enhance parents’ understanding of mental health and their own psychological well-being, and strengthen their knowledge and ability to early identify and support children with mental health problems, offering about 3,000 places every year.
The bureau also highlighted under-reporting of cases, as some parents and students hesitated to let schools submit their information to the government.
It also said that the number of suspected suicide cases among primary and secondary school students rose by 10 per cent between 2024 and last year, from 28 to 31.
If you have suicidal thoughts or know someone who is experiencing them, help is available. In Hong Kong, you can dial 18111 for the government-run Mental Health Support Hotline.
You can also call +852 2896 0000 for The Samaritans or +852 2382 0000 for Suicide Prevention Services. In the US, call or text 988 or chat at 988lifeline.org for the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline. For a list of other nations’ helplines, see this page.

data.title: BrainCo’s brain-computer interface wows at HSBC summit with mind-controlled hand
data.description:
Non-invasive brain-computer interfaces (BCI) that read neural signals through the skin could help patients manage symptoms for some brain conditions, according to BrainCo, a Harvard-incubated start-up.
BrainCo’s BCI technology could “read brain neural signals and translate them into commands to control machines to modulate brain function and help cure some brain diseases”, said Nyx He, a partner and senior vice-president of BrainCo, at the HSBC Global Investment Summit on Thursday.
The 11-year-old firm had developed algorithms to analyse and understand those signals, she said.
“For severe brain issues like Parkinson’s disease, or for blind people who want to recover sight, you may have to go through surgery,” He said. “But there are also many other applications where you don’t need surgery, like what we do.”

BrainCo conducted a small demonstration at the summit. A man wearing a bionic hand appeared on stage to show how the company’s device reacts to neural signals by moving his index finger and other digits using only his thoughts. BrainCo claimed that it was the first prosthetic hand in the world that could control each finger individually.
He then used the same prosthesis to play a short piece on a digital piano, showing how the device could handle more complex actions, drawing warm applause from the audience.
“The sensors only touch the skin and read the neural signals under the skin [and then] the muscles contract to move the hand,” He said.
Compared with Musk’s Neuralink, which relies on brain surgery to implant electrodes and read signals from the cortex, BrainCo had chosen a non-invasive route that was “safer, easier for patients to accept and reduces both risk and cost”, He said.
The company’s technology could also be used to improve people’s focus levels and help users to improve their sleep, she added.
BrainCo was founded in the US by Han Bicheng, who went to Harvard University, and He, who represented the company at the summit, holds a law degree from Harvard Law School, according to their LinkedIn profiles.
The start-up was incubated at Harvard Innovation Labs. It also operates in Hangzhou, the capital of east China’s Zhejiang province, and is one of the so-called “Six Little Dragons” – an informal grouping of six Hangzhou-based tech firms that have risen to global prominence in artificial intelligence, robotics and other technologies.
BrainCo launched its Asia-Pacific headquarters and research and development base at Hong Kong’s Cyberport last year following Hong Kong Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu’s visit to the company’s Hangzhou facility.
BrainCo has raised about US$292 million from investors including Hong Kong-based IDG Capital, San Francisco-based Walden International and Shanghai-based Hantan Capital, according to Tracxn, a market intelligence platform.
The company confidentially filed for a Hong Kong initial public offering, Bloomberg reported on January 11.
Last month, China’s BCI sector notched a major regulatory milestone. Shanghai-based Neuracle Medical Technology became the first company in the world to receive commercial approval for an implantable BCI system – a coin-sized device designed to restore hand motor function in patients with spinal cord injuries.
Beijing has set an ambitious target to cultivate “two or three” globally competitive BCI companies by 2030, positioning Chinese firms as direct rivals to Musk’s Neuralink.

data.title: We failed to check if workers smoked at Wang Fuk Court, firms tell fire hearing
data.description:
Two renovation subcontractors involved in a HK$336 million (US$42.9 million) project at a subsidised estate ravaged by Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in decades have conceded they did not regulate workers’ smoking behaviour before the inferno.
The two engineering firms on Thursday also told an independent committee that they had partially replaced fireproof windows at Wang Fuk Court’s evacuation passages with movable wooden boards to allow workers’ easy access to scaffolding at the behest of main contractor Prestige Construction and Engineering.
Ng Pui-kwan, whose company received more than HK$8 million for works carried out at three of eight towers at the housing estate, acknowledged that the move was unnecessary. But he said he had not known that removing the windows would pose a danger to the buildings.
The committee previously heard that toxic smoke could have gushed into the buildings’ emergency staircases through the temporary openings and prevented some residents from escaping.
Ng said he had told his workers not to smoke on bamboo scaffolding, but had no idea if they had followed his instructions.
The witness also admitted to using polyfoam boards to cover windows for the first time during Wang Fuk Court’s project without knowing if they were fire-retardant.
Zhang Minghai, whose company was in charge of Wang Tao House’s renovation, conceded that he only visited the site at the end of each day and had never taken proactive measures to ban workers from smoking on the bamboo structure.


Lee Kwok-hung, whose mother and their two domestic helpers were among the 168 people killed in the blaze, said he had raised his concerns with workers, only to be told that they were chosen through the Urban Renewal Authority’s Smart Tender system and were subject to government oversight.
“Where is the oversight?” he asked.
The committee on Thursday also heard further accusations against Tai Po district councillor Peggy Wong Pik-kiu for allegedly collecting residents’ proxy votes to help Prestige secure the lucrative project.
Danny Fung Yiu, a former member of the estate’s owners’ corporation management committee, accused Wong of exerting undue influence over the decision to adopt Prestige’s proposal, the most expensive among 57 options, for Wang Fuk Court’s exterior overhaul in January 2024.
The 67-year-old resident said Wong, of the pro-establishment Democratic Alliance for the Betterment and Progress of Hong Kong (DAB), had previously brought along her “henchmen” to attend flatowners’ meetings and intimidate participants who disagreed with them.
On the day residents voted for the contractor, Wong’s supporters allegedly lined up outside the assembly hall to collect residents’ authorisation documents.
The voting started as soon as all of Wong’s supporters entered the venue, even as other residents were still waiting to get in, Fung added.
Kenneth Tai Chi-man, who lived at Wang Chi House, said his mother was lured into consenting to Wong’s associates to vote on her behalf in an election for the management committee’s chairman.
He said DAB volunteers had told his mother they could assist her in applying for government subsidies.
“Upon knowing that, I immediately rushed down to retrieve the proxy, but that was done after many difficulties,” he said.

Tai, who works in the engineering sector, also highlighted the project’s “unreasonable” aspects, such as Prestige’s request to remove air conditioners from flats for a year and blocking residents’ windows.
He said he had relayed his concerns to Prestige director Hau Wa-kin, but the latter said they had to conduct the procedure across eight blocks simultaneously due to a lack of funding.
“I yelled at him, saying they were not volunteers and we had paid them,” Tai said.
“We usually charge HK$600 for removing and reinstalling an air-conditioner, but they charged HK$4,500 for three. You can see how wide the margin is.”
Tai also accused Tony Tsui Moon-come, who became the management committee’s chairman after the project started, of turning a deaf ear to residents’ appeals to terminate Prestige’s contract.
Outside the hearing, Tai said that Wong, as a public official, should have remained neutral about the project without favouring any side.
“It’s ridiculous,” the witness told reporters. “How could you, as a district councillor, help the management committee to collect proxy votes? Did she tell flatowners how she would vote? She didn’t.”
He said no residents had ever seen Wong visiting the residential complex after the fire, and urged her to testify before the independent committee and explain her actions.
Tsui and another witness are expected to give evidence when the hearing resumes at City Gallery in Central on Friday.

data.title: Hong Kong police bust beautician-led syndicate behind HK$410,000 credit card theft
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested a syndicate led by a beautician that allegedly stole credit cards from patrons of a beauty salon in Mong Kok, causing losses of more than HK$410,000 (US$52,347).
The force said on Thursday that four men and two women, aged between 39 and 52, had been arrested over the past two days. One suspect was allegedly found with stolen credit cards in their possession at the time.
Between last December and April, police received reports from 19 residents who claimed their credit cards had been stolen and used for high-value transactions, said Superintendent Li Muk-yik of the southern New Territories regional headquarters.
Investigations revealed that all of the victims had visited the same parlour in the Mong Kok district.
Li said customers at the beauty parlour often left their personal belongings nearby and became less vigilant during treatments.
The force believes the 39-year-old beautician was the mastermind behind the crimes.
“The beautician will take advantage of when patrons are lying down and relaxed with their eyes closed, to take their credit cards from their wallets,” Li said.
She would then pass the credit cards to her accomplice waiting nearby, and they would go to electronics, gold and Chinese herbal medicine shops to make high-value purchases such as mobile phones, herbs or supplements, which were later resold for cash, he added.

Li said the largest loss in a single case was about HK$50,000.
“The beautician would only target credit cards inside their wallets and put the wallets back in place immediately to avoid detection,” he said.
“The victims would not find out until much later, or when they received their banking statements and realised unauthorised transactions were made.”
Li said members of the syndicate had clear roles, from stealing credit cards to making fraudulent purchases and reselling the goods, adding that their operation was “very cunning”.
Three of the suspects were charged with theft and obtaining property by deception, and their cases were mentioned at Sha Tin Court on Thursday.
Li reminded the public to store their personal belongings in lockers at places such as gyms or beauty parlours, rather than leaving them out in the open out of habit.

data.title: Chinese investors cheer Tesla’s AI chip progress, boosting shares of suppliers
data.description:
Chinese suppliers of parts for Tesla’s electric vehicles (EVs) and robots saw their stock prices gain on Thursday morning as the US tech giant announced that its AI5 artificial intelligence inference chip was 45 days ahead of schedule.
After a nearly 7.7 per cent jump in Tesla’s Nasdaq-listed stock on Wednesday, shares of the company’s Chinese suppliers were up by as much as 4.6 per cent on Thursday morning local time.
Shanghai-listed Ningbo Tuopu Group and Shenzhen-listed Zhejiang Sanhua Intelligent Controls, which both announced their relationship with the US firm back in 2016, saw their shares rise as much as 4.3 per cent and 3.4 per cent, respectively.
Both firms are believed to be seeking to expand their partnership with Tesla, from supplying EV parts to robotics components. In 2024, they announced separate plans worth billions of yuan each to invest in robotics production, although they did not name any customer.

Shanghai Beite Technology saw the largest share spike among suppliers, up as much as 4.6 per cent on Thursday morning. The company produces shock absorber rods for the Tesla Model 3 through a tier-1 supplier to the US EV giant.
Yinbang Clad Material, which indirectly works with Tesla through Zhejiang Sanhua and France’s Valeo for the Model 3 and Model Y, saw its shares rise as much as 2.8 per cent.
Tesla, whose Gigafactory in Shanghai is its largest production base, currently works with more than 400 Chinese suppliers, and more than 60 have entered Tesla’s global supply chain, the company’s China president Allan Wang Hao said at a press briefing on Tuesday. However, not every supplier is publicly listed or has disclosed their relationship with Tesla.
The positive market sentiment followed Tesla chief executive Elon Musk’s announcement on X on Wednesday that the company’s AI5 chip had completed tape-out, the final step in the design phase before manufacturing. It was an “inference chip for autonomy” that would enter production in 2027, targeting a 50-fold improvement in performance compared with the previous generation, according to Tesla’s shareholder update for the fourth quarter of 2025.
In January, Musk posted on X that the AI5 would be “very capable” and that two of them could match the performance of a single Nvidia Blackwell chip, but “cost peanuts and use much less power”.
He also revealed on Wednesday an update on the AI6 chip, the Dojo3 system for AI training and “other exciting chips in work”.
Musk tagged Samsung Electronics and Taiwan Semiconductor Co, a different company from foundry giant Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC), as manufacturing partners for the AI5, although it is widely believed that TSMC was part of the deal as Taiwan Semiconductor only makes passive components such as rectifiers and diodes.
Shares of Samsung and TSMC rose 3 per cent and 0.2 per cent, respectively, on Thursday.
Additional reporting by Wency Chen

data.title: Sigenergy jumps 103% in Hong Kong debut on rising energy storage demand
data.description:
Shares of Chinese energy storage system (ESS) maker Sigenergy Technology jumped 103 per cent on their Hong Kong trading debut, as concerns surrounding energy security due to the conflict in the Middle East boost demand for renewable-energy solutions.
The Shanghai-based company’s shares opened at HK$581, versus its offer price of HK$324.20. It climbed further and closed at HK$659.50. The firm raised HK$4.4 billion (US$560 million) from its initial public offering.
On the grey market on Wednesday night, Sigenergy’s shares surged between 78.3 per cent and 84.5 per cent on major brokerage platforms in the city, after the retail tranche was oversubscribed 1,102 times during the five-day share sale period that ended on Monday.
Established in 2022, Sigenergy was founded by Xu Yingtong, a former Huawei Technologies executive who had been with the company for 23 years.
Sigenergy, which offers systems that integrate solar power, battery energy storage and electric vehicle charging, mainly for households, was ranked the world’s largest provider of stackable all-in-one distributed ESS solutions, as measured by product shipments, with a 28.6 per cent market share in 2024, according to its prospectus.
Exports accounted for 99 per cent of Sigenergy’s total revenue, thanks to its sales network spanning 85 countries and regions by the end of 2025.
An ESS refers to a device that converts energy from power-generating systems into a form that can be stored and later converted back into electricity when needed.
The global ESS market has grown rapidly amid surging demand for renewable energy in the last few years. From 2020 to 2024, global shipments exploded from 14.2 gigawatt-hours to 230GWh; by 2030, the volume is expected to increase further to 804.5GWh, according to Frost & Sullivan.
In 2025, Sigenergy posted 9 billion yuan (US$1.3 billion) in revenue and 2.92 billion yuan in net profit, up 577 per cent and 3,381 per cent respectively, from a year earlier.
Besides its wide sales network, Sigenergy also attributed the rapid growth to the continuing industry growth trends and favourable government policies that are driving demand for ESS solutions.
Sigenergy is a darling of institutional investors, with Temasek’s Aranda Investments, Goldman Sachs Asset Management (Hong Kong), and Asset Management (Singapore) among the 19 cornerstone investors that committed US$280 million worth of shares.
The ESS firm said in the prospectus that it planned to allocate 38 per cent of the net proceeds to expand its research and development team and upgrade equipment and technology, and 32 per cent to strengthen marketing and after-sales service to drive its global expansion.
Iran’s war with the US and Israel has sent oil and natural gas prices soaring, sparking demand for energy alternatives.
Brent crude prices have surged over 34 per cent from the pre-conflict level to around US$94 per barrel this week, after crossing US$110 last month.
China, which leads the world in ramping up electricity capacity, has seen orders increase for grid equipment and batteries recently, pushing mainland manufacturers to expand their production capacity.

data.title: ASML boosts 2026 sales forecast despite shrinking China sales
data.description:
Dutch chipmaker ASML upgraded its 2026 sales outlook on Wednesday, citing strong demand, even as China’s share of global shipments continued to decline and the firm braced for a potential new round of US export controls targeting China.
The company projected net sales for the year to fall between €36 billion (US$42.5 billion) and €40 billion, as it released its first-quarter results, up from its previous forecast of between €34 billion and €39 billion made in January.
The semiconductor industry’s momentum was picking up, fuelled by investments in artificial intelligence infrastructure, which were creating strong demand for advanced memory and logic chips, according to CEO Christophe Fouquet.
“We expect that supply will not meet demand for the foreseeable future,” Fouquet said.

ASML’s customers on the memory side were saying they were already sold out for the year, while customers on the advanced logic side continued to build capacity for the AI boom, he said.
The firm said it would continue to work closely with its customers to increase production capacity in 2027.
Sales of the group’s machines reached €6.3 billion during the first quarter, while China’s share of global shipments dropped to 19 per cent, from 36 per cent in the fourth quarter of 2025, according to ASML’s financial statement. South Korea replaced China as ASML’s biggest market, accounting for 45 per cent of its sales, compared with 22 per cent in the same period.
The decline was in line with ASML’s expectations, as CFO Roger Dassen said in January that he expected China’s share would drop to around 20 per cent this year.
Dassen also clarified during a call with analysts on Wednesday that growth in the firm’s non-EUV (extreme ultraviolet light) machine sales – the lower end of the market open to Chinese buyers – did not come from China. He added that his earlier expectation of Chinese sales accounting for roughly 20 per cent of total demand still held.
The upgraded outlook will also give the Dutch firm enough headroom to absorb the impact of a potential new round of US export controls targeting China.
The Match Act, introduced on April 2 by Republican representative Michael Baumgartner, would require allied nations to align their semiconductor equipment export restrictions on China with US curbs within 150 days, targeting what the bill described as loopholes in existing controls.
“We believe we can accommodate potential outcomes of the export control discussions that are currently ongoing,” Dassen said.

data.title: Greater Bay Airlines suspends Bangkok flights amid fuel price surge
data.description:
Greater Bay Airlines has suspended its flights between Hong Kong and Bangkok for more than four months amid surging fuel prices driven by the conflict in the Middle East.
The airline’s website on Thursday showed no flights from Hong Kong to Bangkok between May 11 and September 22, and none from Bangkok to Hong Kong between May 12 and September 30.
The carrier said it had adjusted its services due to rising fuel prices and apologised to affected travellers.
“Due to the continued rise in fuel prices and the constantly changing market environment, Greater Bay Airlines has made short-term adjustments to services on certain routes,” the airline said.
“We will proactively contact the affected passengers and make appropriate arrangements to minimise any inconvenience caused to them. Greater Bay Airlines sincerely apologises to the affected passengers.”

The United States-Israel war on Iran has continued to affect airlines, with oil markets remaining volatile since the conflict broke out at the end of February and led to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which 20 per cent of the world’s oil supply passes.
Last week, Hong Kong flag carrier Cathay Pacific Airways announced it would cut about 2 per cent of its total flight frequencies between May 16 and June 30, mainly on regional routes, as well as a small number of services to Australia, South Asia and South Africa.
Cathay’s budget arm, HK Express, will also cancel about 6 per cent of flights from May 11 to June 30.
The company said reducing capacity had been a “last resort”, but it needed to consolidate passenger flights to absorb part of the increased costs.
Cathay added that it had sought to maintain normal operations, including by raising fuel surcharges, but the measures were not enough to offset higher costs.

data.title: Hong Kong reports 99% participation of young 2019 protest convicts in rehab project
data.description:
Nearly 1,800 young Hongkongers convicted of protest and national security-related offences have taken part in a rehabilitation programme, authorities have said, including 99 per cent of those found guilty over the 2019 unrest, up from 63 per cent in 2021.
Responding to a question from lawmaker Chan Hok-fung, the Security Bureau said that 1,768 inmates had taken part in Project PATH as of the end of February.
“According to the observation and assessment made by correctional officers, Project PATH has yielded satisfactory results with most participants giving positive response by actively taking part in activities under the programme,” Apollonia Liu Lee Ho-kei, permanent secretary of the Security Bureau, said in her reply.
The rehabilitation programme, launched in November 2021, targets “radical” young offenders arrested during the 2019 anti-government protests or under the city’s national security laws.
Participants take part in activities designed to deepen their understanding of Chinese culture, rebuild their values and strengthen family relationships.
The Correctional Services Department also organised mainland exchange tours in February for discharged participants to enhance their understanding of the country and boost their sense of national identity, with more planned for later this year.
Liu said the participation rate of convicted young offenders linked to the 2019 protests had risen to 99 per cent as of February, up from 63 per cent in 2021.
“Since participation in the programme is entirely voluntary, the significant increase in the participation rate shows that it is well recognised by relevant persons in custody,” she said.
Police said 10,279 people aged 11 to 87 were arrested over the 2019 protests. As of September 2024, 2,974 had faced or entered judicial proceedings, while more than 7,000 had yet to be charged.
The recidivism rate among the 470 former inmates who joined the programme and had been released for two years or more stood at 0.2 per cent.
This rate, referring to the likelihood of former inmates reoffending within two years of release, was far lower than the 22.4 per cent recorded for other offenders.
“However, since participation in the activity is entirely voluntary, specific arrangements will depend on the number of applications received and the overall allocation of resources,” Liu added.
Earlier this month, Cantopop singer Hins Cheung announced that he would lead young people arrested during the 2019 protests but not charged on visits to mainland China to witness the country’s development and serve as a mentor for a “special rehabilitation project” under the Security Bureau.

In a separate response, the bureau said that 267 people were serving sentences for offences related to the protests or national security cases as of March 1, while 25 people were on remand for similar offences.
Their prison terms ranged from 84 days to 23 years and 10 months, with no data available on the average time under custody.
Of those currently in custody, 181 had been convicted of rioting, 63 had violated the national security law or the Safeguarding National Security Ordinance, and 48 had been convicted of other offences such as unlawful assembly and arson.
A total of seven inmates were aged under 21.

data.title: Macau finance minister steps down for ‘personal reasons’
data.description:
Macau’s finance minister has resigned over “personal reasons,” prompting the city’s chief executive to temporarily assume his duties until a new candidate is appointed.
The cabinet change took effect on Thursday after China’s State Council approved the removal of Tai Kin-ip from the post of secretary for economy and finance, in accordance with Macau’s Basic Law and the recommendation of Chief Executive Sam Hou-fai.
Tai, 57, who only assumed the senior role in December 2024, said he had earlier submitted his resignation to Sam for “personal reasons,” according to a government press statement.
“I will, as always, continue to care for and support the chief executive and the government in governing according to the law, and contribute to Macau’s various development initiatives,” he said.
He also expressed gratitude to the central government and to Sam for their trust and support, as well as to civil servants in the economic and financial sectors for their dedication and cooperation.
In a separate statement, Sam thanked Tai for his contributions and hard work.
“Tai has consistently implemented the government’s governance philosophies and policy plans, leading his team to govern responsibly and in accordance with the law, while driving new progress in Macau’s economic and financial sectors,” Sam said.
He added that the government was proceeding with the nomination process for a new secretary and would request the central government to appoint the candidate at an appropriate time.
Citing the Basic Law, the government said Sam would exercise the powers and duties of the finance minister until the new appointee was inaugurated.
Before taking up the position in December 2024, Tai had served as director of the Economic and Technological Development Bureau, formerly the Economic Bureau, since 2016.
Having first joined the bureau in 1995, he was promoted to deputy director in 2000 before taking the helm in 2016.

data.title: Mark Mobius, pioneering investor in China, dies at 89
data.description:
Mark Mobius, who was known as the father of emerging markets and was among the first fund managers to invest in Hong Kong and mainland China, died on Wednesday at the age of 89, according to a statement on his LinkedIn account.
The statement did not give the cause. Mobius was based in Dubai in recent years and remained active in investment via his own firm. He was managing director of Mobius Emerging Opportunities Fund.
Mobius remained bullish on China throughout his career. In his last interview with the South China Morning Post in November 2024 – for the 10th anniversary of the Stock Connect scheme for bidirectional, cross-border trading – he highlighted Hong Kong’s role as a connector between mainland China and the world.
“The Stock Connect system is better than the QFII [qualified foreign institutional investor programme] since it is easier and requires no specific approvals,” Mobius said. “We have been seeing more Chinese stocks pop up in our scans since the launch of the connect scheme.”
Mobius was never an office-bound investor, preferring for decades to conduct on-site research in China and many emerging markets – a habit that continued into his old age.
Such first-hand experiences helped him see opportunities in China that many of his peers missed. In February 2024, he said China stocks were “not attractive”. But a month later, after a trip to southern China where he saw signs of recovery due to government support measures, he said he believed they were “beginning to look good”.
“China’s stock markets went too low, and people were overly pessimistic,” he said at the time. “When you invest, you do not want to step in front of a high-speed train coming at you, so you step aside and let the train pass.
“When the market reaches the bottom, then you can begin to invest. Now, the Chinese market has reached the bottom and is beginning to deliver the recovery after all the government measures to support the real estate market. We have seen light at the end of the tunnel.”
The veteran fund manager’s family background had nothing to do with finance but did give him exposure to different cultures. His father, Paul Mobius, was a cook and baker who worked on ships sailing between Europe and the United States. His mother, Maria, was from Puerto Rico, and his parents moved to New York after they married.
He studied fine arts, music, theatre, psychology, journalism and economics and eventually chose fund management as his career after completing his university studies.

“I am interested in many things and many different cultures,” he said in an interview with the SCMP in 2016. “I found out that if you are a fund manager, you can visit many different countries and study many different companies, which was what I liked to do.”
On one such trip, he arrived in Hong Kong in 1967. He set up a research and consulting firm in the city and visited mainland China from time to time.
He began shaving his head at that time, after a fire burned his hair, which became a trademark, prompting comparisons to film star Yul Brynner.
Mobius sold his research firm in Hong Kong after 15 years and then worked for several investment companies, covering Asia, until he was almost 50. He then got a call from fund manager John Templeton asking him to set up Templeton’s first emerging market fund for the American public, where he worked as chairman.
The fund he established with US$100 million in 1987 rose to US$40 billion at its peak in 2007 before falling to US$26 billion in 2016. Mobius was particularly proud of making investments during the Asian financial crisis in 1998 and reaping strong returns when the market bounced back.
The firm, now Franklin Templeton, has US$1.68 trillion in assets under management.
Mobius stepped down as chairman of Templeton Emerging Markets Group in January 2018. A few years ago he set up Mobius Investments, which issued the statement announcing his death. Mobius Investments partners John Ninia and Eric Nguyen would lead the firm now, the statement said.

data.title: Tai Po fire probe: resident recalls deaths of 3 family members, domestic helper
data.description:
In his opening statement, committee lead counsel Victor Daws identified six “human factors” behind the inferno’s high death toll.
These included the deactivation of the fire alarm system, the inactive fire hose system, the use of flammable polyfoam boards to protect windows during renovation, and the use of non-fire-retardant scaffolding mesh.
Other factors cited were the replacement of fireproof windows at emergency staircases with wooden movable boards to allow workers access to scaffolding, and workers’ smoking habits at the site.
